explanation:
In 2001, 17 software practitioners met and wrote the Agile Manifesto — four values and twelve principles that revolutionized how teams build software. Before Agile, teams spent months writing detailed specifications before writing a single line of code. The result? Products that were outdated before they shipped.

The four values prioritize: individuals and interactions over processes and tools, working software over comprehensive documentation, customer collaboration over contract negotiation, and responding to change over following a plan. The twelve principles expand on these with practices like frequent delivery, sustainable pace, and continuous improvement.
example:
```python
# Simulating Waterfall vs Agile approaches

# WATERFALL APPROACH (rigid, sequential)
waterfall_phases = [
    {"phase": "Requirements", "duration": "3 months", "output": "200-page spec"},
    {"phase": "Design", "duration": "2 months", "output": "Architecture document"},
    {"phase": "Implementation", "duration": "6 months", "output": "Code"},
    {"phase": "Testing", "duration": "2 months", "output": "Bug reports"},
    {"phase": "Deployment", "duration": "1 month", "output": "Shipped product"},
]
print("=== WATERFALL ===")
for phase in waterfall_phases:
    print(f"  {phase['phase']:20s} | {phase['duration']:10s} | {phase['output']}")
print(f"  Total: 14 months to first user feedback\n")

# AGILE APPROACH (iterative, adaptive)
agile_sprints = [
    {"sprint": 1, "duration": "2 weeks", "deliverable": "Login + user profile"},
    {"sprint": 2, "duration": "2 weeks", "deliverable": "Dashboard + notifications"},
    {"sprint": 3, "duration": "2 weeks", "deliverable": "Search + filtering"},
    {"sprint": 4, "duration": "2 weeks", "deliverable": "Payment integration"},
]
print("=== AGILE ===")
for sprint in agile_sprints:
    print(f"  Sprint {sprint['sprint']} | {sprint['duration']:10s} | {sprint['deliverable']}")
print(f"  Total: 8 weeks, feedback after each sprint\n")

# The 4 Agile Values
def compare_values():
    values = [
        ("Individuals and interactions", "over", "processes and tools"),
        ("Working software", "over", "comprehensive documentation"),
        ("Customer collaboration", "over", "contract negotiation"),
        ("Responding to change", "over", "following a plan"),
    ]
    print("=== AGILE VALUES ===")
    for left, arrow, right in values:
        print(f"  {left:35s} {arrow:5s} {right}")
        print(f"  (While the right items have value, we value the left items more)")

compare_values()
```
key_takeaway: Agile values working software, customer collaboration, and adaptability over rigid plans — deliver small increments frequently and respond to feedback.
try_it: Write a function that simulates a 6-sprint project where each sprint's scope can change based on 'customer feedback' (random input). Show how Agile adapts while Waterfall doesn't.

explanation:
Scrum is the most popular Agile framework. It organizes work into fixed-length iterations (sprints, usually 2 weeks) with defined roles: Product Owner (what to build), Scrum Master (how to work), and Development Team (build it). Think of it as a sports team with a coach, captain, and players.

Each sprint follows a cycle: Sprint Planning (select work), Daily Standup (15-min sync), Sprint Review (demo to stakeholders), and Sprint Retrospective (improve the process). The Product Backlog is a prioritized list of features; the Sprint Backlog is the subset selected for the current sprint.
key_takeaway: Scrum organizes work into 2-week sprints with clear roles — Product Owner prioritizes, Scrum Master facilitates, and the team delivers working software every iteration.
try_it: Add a `SprintRetrospective` method that collects 'went well', 'improve', and 'action items' from team members and stores them in the sprint history.

explanation:
Kanban visualizes work on a board with columns representing workflow stages (To Do, In Progress, Done). Unlike Scrum's fixed sprints, Kanban is continuous — work flows through the system without iteration boundaries. Think of it as a highway: cars (tasks) move through lanes (stages) with speed limits (WIP limits).

The key principles are: visualize work, limit work-in-progress (WIP), manage flow, make policies explicit, implement feedback loops, and improve collaboratively. WIP limits prevent multitasking — if the 'In Progress' column has a limit of 3, you can't start a new task until one finishes.
key_takeaway: Kanban visualizes workflow with WIP limits — work flows continuously without sprints, and WIP limits prevent multitasking bottlenecks.
try_it: Add a `blocked` flag to cards and a `Blocked` column. When a card is blocked, move it to the Blocked column and don't count it against the WIP limit of In Progress.

explanation:
User stories capture requirements from the user's perspective: 'As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit].' They're conversation starters, not contracts — the details emerge through dialogue between the team and stakeholders. Acceptance criteria define 'done' for each story.

Estimation uses relative sizing (story points) instead of absolute time. A 5-point story is roughly twice as complex as a 2-point story. Planning Poker is the standard technique: team members independently reveal their estimates, discuss outliers, and converge. This leverages collective wisdom and surfaces hidden complexity.
example:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import statistics

@dataclass
class AcceptanceCriteria:
    description: str
    met: bool = False

@dataclass
class UserStory:
    id: int
    role: str
    action: str
    benefit: str
    story_points: Optional[int] = None
    acceptance_criteria: list = field(default_factory=list)
    priority: str = "medium"

    @property
    def title(self) -> str:
        return f"As a {self.role}, I want {self.action}, so that {self.benefit}"

    @property
    def is_ready(self) -> bool:
        return self.story_points is not None and len(self.acceptance_criteria) > 0

    @property
    def is_done(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_ready and all(ac.met for ac in self.acceptance_criteria)

    def __str__(self):
        status = "DONE" if self.is_done else "IN PROGRESS" if self.is_ready else "NOT READY"
        return f"[{status}] {self.title} ({self.story_points or '?'} pts)"

class PlanningPoker:
    FIBONACCI = [1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21]

    def __init__(self, team_members: list[str]):
        self.team = team_members

    def estimate(self, story: UserStory, estimates: dict[str, int]) -> int:
        """Run a planning poker session for a user story."""
        print(f"\n=== Planning Poker: {story.title} ===")
        print(f"Team estimates:")
        for member, estimate in estimates.items():
            print(f"  {member}: {estimate} points")

        values = list(estimates.values())
        avg = statistics.mean(values)
        median = statistics.median(values)

        # Check for outliers (discuss if spread > 50% of median)
        spread = max(values) - min(values)
        if spread > median * 0.5:
            print(f"\n  Wide spread detected ({spread} points). Discussing...")
            highest = max(estimates, key=estimates.get)
            lowest = min(estimates, key=estimates.get)
            print(f"  {highest} estimated {estimates[highest]}, {lowest} estimated {estimates[lowest]}")
            print(f"  Team discusses and converges on: {int(median)}")

        # Round to nearest Fibonacci
        closest = min(self.FIBONACCI, key=lambda f: abs(f - median))
        story.story_points = closest
        print(f"  Final estimate: {closest} story points")
        return closest

# Create user stories
login_story = UserStory(
    id=1,
    role="user",
    action="log in with my Google account",
    benefit="I don't need to remember another password",
    priority="high"
)
login_story.acceptance_criteria = [
    AcceptanceCriteria("Google OAuth button appears on login page"),
    AcceptanceCriteria("Clicking button redirects to Google consent screen"),
    AcceptanceCriteria("After consent, user is logged in and redirected to dashboard"),
    AcceptanceCriteria("New user record is created if first login"),
]

# Run planning poker
poker = PlanningPoker(["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana"])
estimates = {"Alice": 5, "Bob": 8, "Charlie": 5, "Diana": 13}
poker.estimate(login_story, estimates)

print(f"\nStory: {login_story}")
print(f"Ready for sprint: {login_story.is_ready}")
print(f"Acceptance criteria:")
for ac in login_story.acceptance_criteria:
    print(f"  [{'x' if ac.met else ' '}] {ac.description}")
```
key_takeaway: User stories capture 'who wants what and why' — use Planning Poker for consensus-based estimation and acceptance criteria to define 'done'.
try_it: Add a `VelocityTracker` class that tracks completed story points across multiple sprints and calculates the team's average velocity for future sprint planning.

explanation:
The retrospective is the most important Agile ceremony — it's where the team inspects its own process and adapts. Without it, you're just going through the motions. Think of it as a sports team watching game film: what worked, what didn't, and what to change for next game.

The classic format is 'Start, Stop, Continue': what should we start doing, stop doing, and keep doing? Other formats include 'Mad, Sad, Glad' (emotional) and '4Ls' (Liked, Learned, Lacked, Longed For). The key is psychological safety — team members must feel safe to speak honestly without blame.
example:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum
import json

class RetroCategory(Enum):
    START = "start"   # Things to begin doing
    STOP = "stop"     # Things to stop doing
    CONTINUE = "continue"  # Things to keep doing

@dataclass
class RetroItem:
    text: str
    category: RetroCategory
    author: str
    votes: int = 0
    action_taken: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class Retrospective:
    sprint_number: int
    date: datetime
    items: list = field(default_factory=list)
    action_items: list = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_item(self, text: str, category: RetroCategory, author: str):
        self.items.append(RetroItem(text, category, author))

    def vote(self, index: int):
        if 0 <= index < len(self.items):
            self.items[index].votes += 1

    def get_top_items(self, n: int = 3) -> dict:
        sorted_items = sorted(self.items, key=lambda x: x.votes, reverse=True)
        result = {}
        for cat in RetroCategory:
            cat_items = [i for i in sorted_items if i.category == cat][:n]
            result[cat.value] = [(i.text, i.votes) for i in cat_items]
        return result

    def create_action_item(self, description: str, owner: str, due_date: str):
        self.action_items.append({
            "description": description,
            "owner": owner,
            "due_date": due_date,
            "status": "open"
        })

    def summary(self) -> str:
        output = f"=== Sprint {self.sprint_number} Retrospective ({self.date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}) ===\n\n"
        top = self.get_top_items()
        for cat, items in top.items():
            output += f"{cat.upper()}:\n"
            for text, votes in items:
                output += f"  [{votes} votes] {text}\n"
            output += "\n"

        if self.action_items:
            output += "ACTION ITEMS:\n"
            for item in self.action_items:
                output += f"  - {item['description']} (Owner: {item['owner']}, Due: {item['due_date']})\n"
        return output

# Run a retrospective
retro = Retrospective(sprint_number=5, date=datetime.now())

# Team adds items
retro.add_item("Daily standups are too long", RetroCategory.STOP, "Alice")
retro.add_item("Pair programming on complex PRs", RetroCategory.START, "Bob")
retro.add_item("Code reviews within 24 hours", RetroCategory.CONTINUE, "Charlie")
retro.add_item("Skip PR reviews for typo fixes", RetroCategory.START, "Diana")
retro.add_item("Working late to meet deadlines", RetroCategory.STOP, "Alice")
retro.add_item("Weekly knowledge sharing sessions", RetroCategory.CONTINUE, "Bob")
retro.add_item("Automated deployment pipeline", RetroCategory.CONTINUE, "Charlie")
retro.add_item("Better error messages in prod", RetroCategory.START, "Diana")

# Voting
for i in [0, 4]:  # Stop items get votes
    retro.vote(i)
    retro.vote(i)
for i in [1, 2, 6]:  # Start/Continue items get votes
    retro.vote(i)

# Create action items from top-voted items
retro.create_action_item(
    "Implement 15-minute timebox for standups",
    owner="Scrum Master",
    due_date="Next sprint"
)
retro.create_action_item(
    "Set up pair programming schedule for complex features",
    owner="Tech Lead",
    due_date="Next sprint"
)

print(retro.summary())
```
key_takeaway: Retrospectives turn feedback into action — vote on what matters most, assign owners to action items, and track them in the next sprint.
try_it: Add a `track_action_items` method that checks which action items from the previous retrospective were completed, and flags overdue ones in the current retrospective.

explanation:
Agile practice focuses on iterative delivery, team collaboration, and continuous improvement. The key patterns are: breaking work into small increments, estimating with relative sizing, visualizing workflow, and conducting effective retrospectives. Master these and you can adapt to any Agile framework — Scrum, Kanban, or SAFe.

The most common Agile interview challenges test your ability to facilitate ceremonies, handle scope changes mid-sprint, and measure team health. Practice by simulating sprints, building Kanban boards, and running mock retrospectives with action item tracking.
example:
```python
# Simulating a sprint retrospective with action item tracking
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def run_retrospective(sprint_num, prev_actions, feedback):
    print(f'=== Sprint {sprint_num} Retrospective ===')
    if prev_actions:
        print('Previous Action Items:')
        for item in prev_actions:
            status = 'DONE' if item['completed'] else 'OVERDUE'
            print(f"  [{status}] {item['description']} (Owner: {item['owner']})")
    categories = {'start': [], 'stop': [], 'continue': []}
    for item in feedback:
        categories[item['category']].append(item)
    for cat in ['start', 'stop', 'continue']:
        items = sorted(categories[cat], key=lambda x: x['votes'], reverse=True)
        print(f'\n{cat.upper()}:')
        for item in items[:3]:
            print(f"  [{item['votes']} votes] {item['text']}")
    new_actions = []
    for cat in ['start', 'stop']:
        top = sorted(categories[cat], key=lambda x: x['votes'], reverse=True)
        if top:
            new_actions.append({'description': top[0]['text'], 'owner': 'Team',
                'due_date': (datetime.now() + timedelta(days=14)).strftime('%Y-%m-%d'),
                'completed': False})
    return new_actions

prev_actions = [{'description': 'Limit standups to 15 min', 'owner': 'SM', 'completed': True}]
feedback = [
    {'text': 'Improve test coverage before merging', 'category': 'start', 'votes': 5},
    {'text': 'Skip daily standups on Fridays', 'category': 'stop', 'votes': 4},
    {'text': 'Maintain code review SLA of 24 hours', 'category': 'continue', 'votes': 6},
]
new_actions = run_retrospective(3, prev_actions, feedback)
```
key_takeaway: Agile ceremonies are only valuable when they produce actionable outcomes — retrospectives must generate tracked action items with owners and deadlines.
try_it: Design a sprint simulation that handles mid-sprint scope changes and measures velocity over 5 sprints.

practice_challenges.title: Build a Sprint Backlog Prioritizer
practice_challenges.hint: Sort by business value per story point to maximize ROI within sprint capacity.
practice_challenges.description: Given a list of user stories with story points and business value, and a sprint capacity, select the optimal set of stories that maximizes total business value without exceeding capacity.

practice_challenges.title: Kanban WIP Limit Violation Detector
practice_challenges.hint: Track card movements and flag when a column exceeds its WIP limit.
practice_challenges.description: Given a Kanban board state with columns and WIP limits, detect which columns are violating their WIP limits and suggest which cards to move.
practice_challenges.example_output: ['To Do exceeds WIP limit by 1 — move card #4 to Backlog']

practice_challenges.title: Velocity Trend Analyzer
practice_challenges.hint: Calculate moving average and standard deviation to detect significant velocity changes.
practice_challenges.description: Given historical velocity data across multiple sprints, detect trends (improving, declining, stable) and forecast the next sprint's capacity with confidence intervals.

practice_challenges.title: Scrum Ceremony Conflict Resolver
practice_challenges.hint: Model each ceremony as a time block with constraints and use interval scheduling.
practice_challenges.description: Given team member availability, ceremony durations, and ordering constraints, schedule all Scrum ceremonies within a 2-week sprint.

practice_challenges.title: Cross-Team Dependency Tracker
practice_challenges.hint: Model team dependencies as a directed graph and detect cycles that would block delivery.
practice_challenges.description: Given multiple teams with inter-team story dependencies, build a dependency graph, detect cycles, identify the critical path, and suggest an optimal release order that minimizes blocked teams.
